refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by tele-operators.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions rather than optimistic average-case assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit latency budgeting and verification.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (command transmission → vehicle sensor acquisition → telemetry reception at operator console) across representative network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) using instrumented test scenarios. Collect p95 statistics over minimum 1000 samples per condition.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and encode vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data) at ≥ 10 Hz minimum sampling rate.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands (movement, tool control) with deterministic latency budget allocation.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and decode remote commands with bounded processing delay.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The network link shall support bidirectional communication with latency contribution ≤ 100 ms p95 under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.5: The teleoperation system shall detect and report latency violations or network degradation to safety services.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency teleoperation architecture with hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding, dedicated network priority queuing, and predictive command buffering to minimize end-to-end latency.
DP-B: Deploy edge computing nodes at network gateway to pre-process telemetry and compress state updates, reducing payload size and transmission latency.
DP-C: Establish latency monitoring and adaptive bitrate control that dynamically adjusts telemetry fidelity based on network conditions to maintain p95 ≤ 150 ms.
DP-D: Implement redundant communication paths (primary + failover) with automatic switchover to maintain latency SLA during network faults.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A + DP-C (integrated): Implement low-latency teleoperation architecture with hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding, dedicated network priority queuing, and predictive command buffering, combined with latency monitoring and adaptive bitrate control that dynamically adjusts telemetry fidelity based on real-time network conditions to maintain p95 ≤ 150 ms.

potential_sources: Coupling between hardware acceleration (DP-A) and adaptive bitrate control (DP-C) is manageable via well-defined codec interfaces and quality-of-service parameters.
Coupling with network link (external) is mitigated by explicit latency budget allocation and monitoring feedback loop.
Coupling with safety services is one-directional (teleoperation reports violations); safety services do not dictate real-time teleoperation parameters.
coupling_check.notes: DP-A provides deterministic low-latency baseline; DP-C adds resilience and adaptability. Combined approach avoids over-specification of network conditions (DP-B edge computing adds unnecessary complexity for this latency target) and single-point-of-failure risk (DP-D redundancy deferred to lower decomposition level if network SLA cannot be met).

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network link can reliably deliver ≤ 100 ms p95 latency under agreed conditions (e.g., 4G/5G with <50 ms RTT, <5% packet loss).
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle hardware supports ≥ 10 Hz sensor sampling and command processing without blocking.
RISK: If network conditions degrade beyond agreed baseline, adaptive bitrate control may reduce telemetry fidelity below operator situational awareness threshold, creating safety hazard.
RISK: Hardware acceleration (GPU/FPGA) introduces procurement, power, and thermal constraints on vehicle platform.
RISK: Predictive command buffering may mask network latency but introduce operator control lag if prediction model is inaccurate.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to include explicit safety thresholds: if adaptive bitrate control cannot maintain p95 ≤ 150 ms AND minimum fidelity requirements, teleoperation shall automatically transition to safe state (e.g., halt vehicle, alert operator). This couples teleoperation DP to safety services DP at design level.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all teleoperation-specific logic: command reception, state encoding/decoding, latency monitoring, adaptive bitrate control, and operator interface buffering. Owns the latency budget and is responsible for meeting p95 ≤ 150 ms constraint end-to-end from operator input to vehicle actuation feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement vectors, tool control signals) from remote operator console. Latency-critical: must be processed with minimal buffering.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, tool status) to operator console. Latency-critical: must be encoded and queued with priority to meet p95 budget.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication channel to remote operator. Teleoperation module monitors latency contribution and adapts bitrate/fidelity based on network conditions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Reports latency violations, network degradation, or teleoperation faults to Safety Services for operator notification and potential safe-state transition.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of latency-critical encoding, decoding, buffering, and monitoring logic. All DP-A and DP-C mechanisms (hardware acceleration, adaptive bitrate, predictive buffering) are contained within this module. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface crosses boundary.

Vehicle module owns sensor acquisition and command actuation, but does not own teleoperation protocol logic, encoding/decoding, or latency monitoring. Assigning DP-A/C to Vehicle would scatter teleoperation concerns across vehicle firmware and create tight coupling to operator interface logic.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and operator alerts, not real-time teleoperation latency control. Assigning DP-A/C to Safety Services would create unacceptable latency overhead and violate separation of concerns.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container but does not own specific teleoperation implementation. Assigning DP-A/C to NGHE Solution would bypass architectural layering and prevent clear ownership and testing.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the correct architectural boundary for DP-A and DP-C. It owns the latency budget, controls encoding/decoding and adaptive bitrate logic, and interfaces with Vehicle (via Tasking/Telemetry) and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert). This containment minimizes diffusion and aligns with operator-centric teleoperation concerns.
containment_risks: RISK: If Vehicle module cannot guarantee ≥ 10 Hz sensor sampling and command processing, Teleoperation module cannot meet latency budget regardless of internal optimization. Requires explicit interface contract with Vehicle.

RISK: If Network Link latency exceeds 100 ms p95 baseline, Teleoperation adaptive bitrate control may degrade telemetry fidelity below safety threshold. Requires escalation to Safety Services and potential safe-state transition.

RISK: Hardware acceleration (GPU/FPGA) for encoding/decoding may be physically located on Vehicle platform but logically owned by Teleoperation module. Requires clear interface definition and integration testing.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define latency budget allocation: Teleoperation module owns 30 ms (encoding + buffering + adaptive control), Vehicle module owns 20 ms (sensor acquisition + command processing), Network Link owns 100 ms (transmission + jitter). Document these allocations in interface specifications and add instrumentation to measure actual latency at each boundary.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A + DP-C directly addresses FR-NGHE-008 by implementing low-latency architecture with hardware acceleration and adaptive control to achieve p95 ≤ 150 ms. DP-C adds resilience to network variability, which is essential for safety-critical teleoperation. No decoupling or independence issues detected.

diffusion_risks: Minimal diffusion: Teleoperation module contains 95% of DP-A/C logic. Only SafetyAlert interface crosses boundary, which is appropriate for safety reporting.
Hardware acceleration (GPU/FPGA) may require Vehicle module integration, but this is a realization detail (IM layer) not a DP-level concern.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the clear architectural owner. Vehicle and Safety Services modules have well-defined, non-overlapping responsibilities. No competing ownership claims.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: instrument Teleoperation module to measure end-to-end latency (command reception → telemetry transmission) and network latency (Network Link contribution). Conduct latency testing under representative network conditions (4G/5G, variable bandwidth/jitter). Collect p95 statistics over ≥1000 samples.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for p95 ≤ 150 ms end-to-end. Sub-allocations to Vehicle (sensor/command latency) and Network Link (transmission latency) are defined in recommended_revision. Safety Services owns escalation policy if latency SLA is violated.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to include explicit safety thresholds and safe-state transition logic (e.g., halt vehicle if telemetry fidelity drops below minimum).
Document latency budget allocation across Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Network Link modules with measurement instrumentation.
Define interface contracts for Tasking (command format, priority queuing) and Telemetry (state encoding, sampling rate) to ensure Vehicle module can meet latency allocations.
Specify adaptive bitrate control algorithm and fidelity thresholds in DP-C to prevent safety hazards from reduced telemetry quality.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and requires further decomposition into sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.5) to address specific latency contributors: sensor acquisition, command transmission, network link, and safety escalation. DP-A + DP-C is sound and well-contained in Teleoperation module, but sub-level decomposition is necessary to allocate latency budgets, define interface contracts, and verify each component. No revisions to current level are required; proceed to next decomposition level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and encode vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data) at ≥ 10 Hz minimum sampling rate.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands (movement, tool control) with deterministic latency budget allocation.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and decode remote commands with bounded processing delay.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The network link shall support bidirectional communication with latency contribution ≤ 100 ms p95 under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.5: The teleoperation system shall detect and report latency violations or network degradation to safety services.

assumptions: Network link can reliably deliver ≤ 100 ms p95 latency under agreed conditions (e.g., 4G/5G with <50 ms RTT, <5% packet loss).
Vehicle hardware supports ≥ 10 Hz sensor sampling and command processing without blocking.
Operator console can receive and display telemetry at ≥ 10 Hz without additional latency overhead.
Hardware acceleration (GPU/FPGA) is available on vehicle platform for real-time encoding/decoding.
Adaptive bitrate control algorithm can dynamically adjust fidelity while maintaining minimum safety thresholds.
open_risks: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed baseline, causing adaptive bitrate control to reduce telemetry fidelity below operator situational awareness threshold.
Hardware acceleration introduces procurement, power, and thermal constraints on vehicle platform.
Predictive command buffering may mask network latency but introduce operator control lag if prediction model is inaccurate.
Vehicle module may not meet ≥ 10 Hz sensor sampling and command processing latency allocation, cascading failure to teleoperation latency SLA.
evidence_needed: Network latency baseline measurements under agreed conditions (4G/5G, variable bandwidth/jitter).
Vehicle sensor sampling rate and command processing latency measurements.
Hardware acceleration (GPU/FPGA) performance benchmarks for encoding/decoding at target resolution and frame rate.
Adaptive bitrate control algorithm design and fidelity threshold specifications.
End-to-end latency testing results (p95 statistics over ≥1000 samples) under representative network conditions.
Safety impact analysis: operator situational awareness requirements vs. minimum telemetry fidelity.
